narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic records, test scores, and family background to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.60, LSAT score of 26, low family income bracket, full-time student status, and lower decile rankings in Year 1 (2nd decile) and Year 3 (1st decile) grades—and predicted a 52% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This close call tipped the scales toward rejection, as the school prioritizes applicants likely to pass and succeed in their legal careers. The three most influential factors in this decision were your undergraduate GPA, your Year 3 school decile ranking, and your family income bracket. Your solid undergraduate GPA stood out positively, well above the typical 3.21 average, and strongly pushed the prediction toward expecting you to pass the bar. In contrast, your Year 3 decile ranking of 1 (far below the average 5.53) had the strongest negative pull, signaling to the model a significant dip in performance that raised concerns about bar exam readiness. Your low family income bracket, while below average, worked in your favor by nudging the prediction toward passing, perhaps reflecting the model's recognition of resilience in students from similar backgrounds. Compared to typical students, your LSAT of 26 is notably lower than the 36.7 average, which didn't help but wasn't the top driver here, while your full-time status aligns with the vast majority (over 90%) and had neutral impact. Overall, the model saw your Year 3 struggles as outweighing your GPA strengths, creating doubt about bar success despite your promising undergraduate record. This isn't a judgment on your potential as an Asian male determined to enter law—many in similar positions have overcome early hurdles. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the average, strengthening your later-year grades through targeted study or additional coursework, and reapplying next cycle with updated transcripts that highlight improvement. Schools value growth stories like yours, so documenting your proactive steps could make a compelling case in a future application.
